photocatalyst.name: TiO2
photocatalyst.role: light harvester and electron-hole pair generator

chemistry.scope_summary: Photocatalytic oxidative degradation of various organic substrates including phenol, methylphenols, chlorophenols, and dimethylphenols using TiO2 photocatalyst. The process involves generation of electron-hole pairs upon UV irradiation, followed by formation of hydroxyl radicals that oxidize organic substrates to ultimately produce CO2 and H2O through complete mineralization.

optimization_table.key_findings: Relative photonic efficiencies can be used to standardize process comparisons; quantum yields should be based on absorbed rather than incident photons

figures.caption: Fig. 1  Sequence of photophysical and photochemical events taking place upon irradiation of a TiO 2  particle with h v  greater than  E g  together with secondary reactions to total mineralization to CO 2 . Phenol is indicated.

Figure 2 illustrates the photonic efﬁciencies as a function of various Degussa P-25 TiO 2  loadings for the initial photodegradation of phenol for a dispersion irradiated at 365 nm; the initial set of rate data between 0.05 and 0.50 g/L loading used to determine the quantum yield for phenol is that reported in Table 1 of the article Part II [43]. The insert illustrates the linear transform of the  | Fig. 2  Dependence of the photonic efﬁciencies  j  for for the initial photodegradation of phenol on the loading of Degussa P-25 titania [TiO 2 ] for irradiation at the wavelength 365  6  10 nm;; the insert depicts the linear transform plotted as  j ¹ 1   vs. [TiO 2 ] ¹ 1 . The intercept from the linear transform gives the limiting photonic efﬁciency  j lim . For conditions, see text.

figure_descriptions: Mechanistic scheme showing photophysical and photochemical events in TiO2 photocatalysis
Optimization plot showing photonic efficiency vs TiO2 loading

notes: This is a review paper focused on methodology and standardization rather than specific batch-to-flow translation. Most quantitative reaction parameters are not provided as this is a theoretical/methodological paper rather than an experimental study. The paper discusses photocatalytic degradation in general terms without specific reaction conditions or flow chemistry applications.
